steps.title: Understand the logical setup
steps.content: Mooney imposed genre divisions — including a "wonder stories" genre defined by geographic specificity — onto Cherokee stories. The passage then states that Cherokee people are not known to have applied genre divisions to their stories before Mooney. The blank follows "There is, therefore," so it must state a conclusion that logically follows from the Cherokee people not using genre divisions.

steps.title: Test each choice against the logic
steps.content:
• "some reason to think that other traditional stories that the Cherokee elders did not share with Mooney would not have met Mooney's criterion for classifying them as "wonder stories" despite including geographical information.": This is about untold stories not fitting Mooney's criteria — not a conclusion that follows from the absence of Cherokee genre divisions. ✗
  • "no evidence for Mooney's conclusion that the Cherokee elders who recounted the stories believed that the geographic details included in some stories were accurate descriptions of the Cherokee homeland.": Whether elders believed geographic details were accurate is unrelated to whether they used genre divisions. ✗
  • "considerable uncertainty about whether Mooney's classifications of the stories shared by the Cherokee elders were influenced by Long's views about which features of a story are most indicative of the genre to which the story belongs.": Long's influence on Mooney's classifications is not addressed in the passage. ✗
  • "no reason to believe that the Cherokee elders who provided the stories would have agreed with Mooney that the inclusion of geographical specificity in some stories marked those stories as different in kind from other stories.": Since Cherokee people didn't use genre divisions at all, there is no basis to assume they would agree that geographic specificity set certain stories apart as a different kind. This follows directly from the passage. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
